upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very shallow, stable boundary layer with a surface temperature of 21.4°C and an 850mb temp of 18°C, yielding a lapse rate of only 2.26°C/km — far below dry adiabatic (9.8) and even moist adiabatic (6.5), indicating a strongly stable, nearly isothermal atmosphere with essentially no convective potential. The inversion appears to clamp the BL tightly, with model BL heights topping out around 350–420m (~1150–1380 ft) at best this afternoon. Upper-level winds at 700mb are light at 10 kts from 265°, and 500mb shows 30 kts from 270°, meaning no significant wind shear hazard at flight altitudes but also confirming thermals will be weak and capped low all day.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. KHAF METARs are notable: consistent 340–350° winds at 19–22 kts with gusts to 27–30 kts observed through the overnight and early morning hours — strong northerly flow at Half Moon Bay indicates an active sea breeze/marine push is not yet established but overnight drainage/offshore flow is fading. KSFO reporting 290° at 20 kts, peak gust 30 kts at 0042Z. Haze reported at KHAF (5–7SM). No AIRMETs, no TFRs flagged.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland thermal hill, SW/W-facing launch
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750 ft is a pure thermal site. It needs a developing valley breeze (generally SW to W) and a boundary layer that rises above launch elevation (~530m MSL) to produce workable thermals. Ideal surface winds are 8–15 kts from the SW/W with a thermal ceiling well above 2000 ft.
sites.todaySetup: The deciding factor today is the extremely stable atmosphere — the lapse rate of 2.26°C/km means thermals simply cannot develop meaningfully. BL heights stay at 265–385m AGL all day, which translates to roughly 300–1260 ft above valley floor — well below the 1750 ft launch elevation for most of the day. Morning winds are variable and light (2–5 kts shifting from S to E to N), providing no consistent valley breeze. The afternoon sees a weak W-NW sea breeze push arrive around 13:00–14:00 (5–8 kts from 295–320°), but with 98% cloud cover forecast from 13:00–15:00 and a BL height barely reaching 385m, any thermal activity is suppressed entirely. CAPE is effectively zero all day. This site has no viable thermal window today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds are light and variable 2–5 kts, shifting from S to E to NE with BL height only 35–265m. No thermal development, no consistent valley breeze — not launchable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind briefly from NNE at 4 kts (11:00), then near-calm from N (12:00), transitioning to W at 3 kts (13:00). BL reaches ~350m but cloud cover surges to 91–98%, killing any solar heating and thermal potential entirely.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: A weak NW sea breeze arrives 5–7 kts from 313–320° by 14:00–17:00, but 98% cloud cover persists through 15:00. BL tops at 385m — still below launch elevation. No viable flying window.

verdict.hazards: BL height below launch elevation, stable inversion, 98% cloud cover midday suppressing all thermals, variable/light winds with no established valley breeze

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch needs thermal development pushing BL above ~180m AGL and a SW/W valley breeze of 8–14 kts. It is more accessible to lower-hour pilots but still entirely thermal-dependent. Light or variable winds mean no soarable lift.
sites.todaySetup: The same stable inversion that grounds the 1750 ft launch is the story here too. BL heights reach 155–335m AGL, just barely clearing the 600 ft (~183m) launch by midday, but with CAPE at zero and a lapse rate of 2.26°C/km there is no convective energy to drive thermals. Morning winds are light (2–3 kts from variable directions — W, NW, SW, N). The sea breeze push arrives by 13:00–14:00 at 5–7 kts from 276–303°, which is a more usable direction for this launch, but 98% cloud cover from 12:00–15:00 prevents solar heating entirely. A brief clearing after 16:00 brings 7.5 kts from 301° and CAPE spikes to 90 J/kg at 600 ft — a tiny signal of instability — but by then the day is essentially done. No viable window.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Variable 2–3 kts from W/NW/SW/N, BL only 35–245m. Light, shifty, and thermally dead — not worth a launch attempt.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind briefly SW-W at 2–6 kts, BL rising to 335m but cloud cover hits 92–98% by noon, blocking all solar input and thermal development.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Sea breeze arrives 6–7.5 kts from WNW-NW (303–321°), clouds thin after 16:00 with 90 J/kg CAPE blip, but it is too late and too shallow for a meaningful thermal flight.

verdict.hazards: Stable inversion with near-zero CAPE most of day, 98% cloud cover noon–3 PM suppressing thermals, light variable morning winds, BL height marginal relative to launch

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 300 ft launch is the most accessible at Ed Levin and requires the least thermal development — BL just needs to clear ~90m AGL. It works best with a steady SW/W breeze of 6–12 kts and even modest solar heating. It is primarily used for sledders and beginner flights when thermals are weak.
sites.todaySetup: The 300 ft launch is in the same stable airmass as the upper launches, but its lower elevation means the BL height does clear it earlier in the day. However, the lapse rate of 2.26°C/km and zero CAPE mean the air above is stable and any lift will be weak at best. Morning winds are W-NW at 3–5 kts (300–307°), which is a usable direction, and the BL reaches 195–245m by 10:00–11:00. Afternoon winds build to 7–8 kts from 293–307° as the sea breeze fills in — the most consistent window of the day. The 98% cloud cover from 12:00–15:00 is still a strong dampener, but at this altitude a brief sled ride or short thermal poke is conceivable in the late afternoon clearing. Expect sledder conditions only; no sustained thermal soaring.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 3–5 kts from W-NW (242–307°), BL at 40–245m. Direction is usable but lift will be sled-ride quality only; thermals not developed.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind goes light 3 kts from W (281°) at 11:00 then builds to 5–8 kts W-WNW by 12:00–14:00; 93–98% cloud cover kills solar heating, making conditions marginal to sled-ride only.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Sea breeze at 7–8 kts from 293–307° is the best window; clouds thin after 16:00 and CAPE reaches 70 J/kg. Short thermal pokes possible but weak — qualified P2s could do sledder flights.

verdict.hazards: Stable inversion limits thermal quality, extended cloud cover through early afternoon, light/variable morning winds, sledder conditions most of day

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W/NW-facing ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a ridge-soaring site powered by onshore sea breeze hitting the coastal bluff. Ideal winds are W to NW at 10–20 kts measured at the site. Below 8 kts the ridge goes flat; above 25 kts it becomes unsafe. Direction is critical — SW through NW is workable, S or E is not.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock has a challenging setup today. The overnight KHAF METARs showed strong northerly flow (340–350° at 19–22 kts gusting 27–30 kts) — a northerly direction that is off-axis for the W-facing bluff and too strong for comfortable flying. As the morning progresses the direction backs toward W-SW (239–275° by 9–12:00) but speeds drop to 2–6 kts, which is too light for reliable ridge soaring. The midday window (12:00–14:00) offers the best combination: 6–7 kts from 279–298° — on the low end of workable but on-axis for the bluff. By 17:00 speeds pick up to 9.5 kts from 305° with 80m winds at 13.5 kts, which is the strongest and most reliable window of the day but arriving late. Cloud cover fluctuates wildly (11–82%) through the day with no marine layer lockout but patchy conditions. Overall, winds are borderline light through midday with a better but brief punch late afternoon.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 2–5 kts from SW-W (239–275°), on-axis direction but too light for reliable ridge soaring. Patchy clouds 11–82%. Not recommended.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window of the day: 6–7 kts from 279–306° at surface with 80m winds reaching 8.5 kts by 13:00. Marginally soarable for experienced ridge pilots; cloud cover variable 19–79%.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 7–9.5 kts from 286–305° by 16:00–17:00 with 80m winds at 9.3–13.5 kts — the strongest and most consistent window. Experienced pilots may find decent soaring 15:00–17:00.

verdict.hazards: Light and variable winds through midday limiting soarability, overnight NNW gusts to 30 kts fading slowly, haze reducing visibility (5–7SM at KHAF), patchy cloud cover, wind speed borderline all day

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak is a thermal site requiring a developing valley breeze from the SW/W as the inland valley heats up. BL height needs to clear the peak (~750m MSL, ~2460 ft) for proper thermal soaring. It works best on days with strong solar heating and a classic afternoon sea breeze cycle.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak faces the same fundamental problem as Ed Levin: the atmosphere is locked in a stable inversion with a 2.26°C/km lapse rate and near-zero CAPE. BL heights model out at 265–385m AGL — far below the 2460 ft MSL peak elevation for the entire day. Morning winds are S to E at 2–5 kts (variable and thermal-unfriendly). By afternoon the sea breeze arrives from the NW at 5–7 kts (315–320°) which is a workable direction for Mission Peak launches, but 98% cloud cover from 13:00–15:00 suppresses all solar heating during the transition window. The 120m level winds (upper shear layer) are notably stronger at 6.9–8.8 kts with variable direction, suggesting some shear in the column. No viable thermal flight is expected today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 2–5 kts from S/SE/NE (101–190°), variable and off-axis. BL at 35–265m, well below peak elevation. Thermally dead.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Variable NNE-N at 2–5 kts, cloud cover builds to 91–98% by noon. BL reaches ~355m but peak is at ~750m MSL. No usable thermal window.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW sea breeze at 5–7 kts by 14:00–17:00 (315–320°), correct direction for Mission Peak launches. Cloud cover 98% persists until 15:00 then clears. Too little, too late — BL still well below peak elevation.

verdict.hazards: BL height well below launch/peak elevation all day, stable inversion with near-zero CAPE, 98% cloud cover through early afternoon killing solar input, variable morning winds off-axis

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Tricky morning — strong NNE gradient winds at 9–12 kts surface (21 kts at 80m) early, rotating to N-NW by midday. 95% cloud cover at 10:00 suppresses thermals. Afternoon clears with W-NW sea breeze 6–7 kts and BL reaching 425m. Still thermally weak but better than today. Wait for afternoon clearing.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Similar story — strong NNE 14.5 kts at 8 AM (80m winds 23 kts), too strong and wrong direction early. Afternoon transition to NW 7 kts with BL at 435m is more promising. Marginal P3 conditions in the late afternoon window.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Morning NNW gradient wind is light at surface (3.7 kts) but 80m winds at 11 kts suggest shear. Afternoon NW sea breeze 7–8 kts with BL at 420–470m. Best of the Ed Levin launches tomorrow — marginal soaring possible 1–5 PM.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Tomorrow looks problematic — 100% cloud cover through midday with potential light precipitation (1% chance). Afternoon clearing possible by 14:00 with W-NW 7.8–9.7 kts. If it clears, late afternoon (3–5 PM) could offer decent ridge soaring at 9.7 kts WNW. Monitor marine layer burn-off.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Strong NNE gradient 9–12 kts early (21 kts at 80m) is dangerous for launch. Afternoon NW sea breeze 6–7 kts with BL at 405–425m. Similar to today — thermally weak, but afternoon window marginally better. P3+ only.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday (Mar 23) looks like the best day of the stretch. BL heights build to 595–680m by afternoon — finally clearing the 1750 ft launch. NW winds 5–7 kts at surface, 80m winds at 4.7–5.5 kts. Zero clouds and zero precip. Classic spring afternoon thermal cycle possible. Best day of the week for Ed Levin high launch.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday improves significantly — BL reaches 595–680m, NW sea breeze 5–7 kts. Clear skies all afternoon. Thermal soaring likely viable 12:00–17:00. Good P2/P3 day.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday looks very flyable. Clear skies, NW 5–7 kts, BL rising through 680m. Strong thermal development expected. Could be punchy for P2s in the afternoon — P3+ recommended for the better cycles.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Monday shows W-NW winds building from 5.7 kts at 10 AM to 6.9 kts at 4 PM (278–283°), with near-zero clouds. Steady on-axis flow should provide reliable ridge soaring all afternoon. Good day for Mussel Rock — P2+ appropriate.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Monday looks promising — BL climbs to 580–640m, NW/W winds at surface 3–4 kts but 80m winds 7.8–10.4 kts showing a developing sea breeze. Clear skies. Moderate thermal soaring possible 12:00–17:00. P3 conditions.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday (Mar 24) continues the improving trend — BL heights reach 760m by 14:00, NW 7–8.5 kts. Afternoon cloud build to 64% at 16:00 could limit late thermals, but midday window (12:00–14:00) looks excellent. Best day of the 4-day outlook for thermal soaring.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday shows BL reaching 760m with NW 7–8.5 kts. Very similar to 1750 ft forecast — good thermal day. Afternoon cloud increase warrants monitoring but midday looks clean.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday mirrors the other Ed Levin launches — BL 760m, NW 7–8.5 kts, clear midday then clouds build at 16:00. Potentially punchy thermals for P2s — P3 recommended in the afternoon.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Tuesday looks solid — W-NW 5.9–8.3 kts at surface with 80m winds reaching 9.8 kts by 16:00. Direction 282–297° is on-axis. Afternoon cloud build (33%) is mild. Good ridge soaring day, likely P2-accessible in the morning moderate cycle.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Tuesday shows BL reaching 725m, light surface winds (2.7–5 kts W-NW) but strong upper wind shear at 80m (9.4–9.7 kts) and 120m (9.3–11.8 kts). This shear could make for punchy, unpredictable thermals. P3+ day — watch for strong cycles and rotor below the ridge.

watchlist: KHAF overnight winds were 19–22 kts gusting 29–30 kts from NNW — monitor morning gradient wind decay at coastal sites before launching
98% cloud cover forecast Ed Levin and Mission Peak 12:00–15:00 today — kills thermal window entirely
Boundary layer height stays well below 1750 ft launch elevation all day today — high launch is a no-go
Monday (Mar 23) and Tuesday (Mar 24) both show significantly better BL development (595–760m) and cleaner skies — plan mid-week flights
Tuesday upper-level wind shear at Mission Peak (80m/120m winds 9–12 kts vs surface 3–5 kts) could produce punchy turbulent thermals — P3+ only
tldr: Today is a wash for thermal flying — a stubborn stable inversion (lapse rate 2.26°C/km) keeps the BL below 400m all day, 98% clouds kill the midday window, and Ed Levin high launch and Mission Peak have no viable window. Mussel Rock and Ed Levin 300 ft are the only marginal options, both best after 2 PM when the weak NW sea breeze fills in. Save your energy — Monday and Tuesday look genuinely good with BL heights clearing 600–760m and clean NW flow.
